8: ego eimi to Alpha kai to Omega legei Kyrios ho Theos ho on kai ho en kai ho erchomenos ho Pantokrator: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, says the Lord God, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty.' Alpha kai Omega: the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet — a comprehensive inclusio encompassing all of reality. The phrase is applied both to God the Father (Revelation 1:8; Revelation 21:6) and to Jesus (Revelation 22:13: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end') — the shared title making the Christological identification with YHWH explicit. Pantokrator (Almighty): the LXX translation of Hebrew El Shaddai and YHWH Tsvaot (LORD of hosts) — here applied to both Father and Son as the supreme governing power over all creation.
17: ego eimi ho protos kai ho eschatos kai ho zon kai egenomen nekros kai idou zon eimi eis tous aionas ton aionon: 'I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore.' The phrase ho protos kai ho eschatos (first and last) is YHWH's self-identification in Isaiah 41:4; Revelation 44:6; Revelation 48:12 — the exclusive divine claim to be both origin and terminus of all history. Jesus applies this YHWH-title to himself in combination with the resurrection claim: 'I died and behold I am alive.' The resurrection is the proof that the crucified one is the living YHWH — the divine identity claim vindicated by rising from death.
1: apokalypsis Iesou Christou: 'Revelation/disclosure of Jesus Christ.' Apokalypsis (G0602) names the genre: an unveiling of what is hidden. The genitive is double: Jesus Christ is both the source (revelation given by/through him) and the content (revelation about him). Dei genesthai en tachei — must happen soon: dei (divine necessity, the language of God's sovereign plan) + en tachei (in swiftness/soon; not 'soon' in the sense of within a calendar, but with urgency — the apocalyptic 'soon' of imminence). Esēmanen (signified/made known by signs): from sēmeion (sign/symbol), implying the book communicates through symbolic-visionary language, not straightforward prediction.
2: emartyresin ton logon tou theou kai tēn martyrian Iesou Christou: 'bore witness to the word of God and to the testimony of Jesus Christ.' Emartyresin (aorist): John's witness is a completed act — he recorded everything he saw. The double genitive martyria Iesou is programmatic: it means both the testimony about Jesus (objective genitive) and the testimony belonging to Jesus/which Jesus himself gives (subjective genitive); the ambiguity is deliberate throughout Revelation.
3: makarios ho anaginōskōn kai hoi akouontes: 'Blessed is the one who reads aloud, and blessed are those who hear.' The singular anaginōskōn (one reading aloud) and plural akouontes (those hearing) reflect early Christian practice: one lector reading the scroll in the assembly, many listeners — the book was composed to be performed. Terountes ta en autē gegrammena: keeping/obeying (present participle, ongoing) what is written — hearing alone is not the blessing; the blessing belongs to those who hear and keep.
4: ho ōn kai ho ēn kai ho erchomenos: 'the one who is and who was and who is to come.' The phrase is syntactically remarkable: instead of ho esomenos (who will be), John writes ho erchomenos (who is coming). God is not merely 'the one who will exist' but 'the one who is coming' — his futurity is active arrival, not passive existence. The seven spirits before the throne (Revelation 1:4) alludes to Isaiah 11:2's sevenfold Spirit; the number seven = completeness/perfection, so this is the fullness of the Holy Spirit's presence.
5: ho martys ho pistos, ho prōtotokos tōn nekrōn, ho archōn tōn basileōn tēs gēs: three titles in a single phrase. Martys (witness): in Revelation, 'witness' carries the weight of one who testifies at cost of life — Jesus is the faithful witness because his testimony led to his death (the semantic development toward 'martyr'). Prōtotokos tōn nekrōn (firstborn of the dead): not born first among the dead but the one who inaugurates resurrection — the 'firstborn' as the one who opens the womb of death (Colossians 1:18 parallel). Ho agapōnti hēmas: present participle — not 'who loved us' (past) but 'the one who loves us' (ongoing present love).
6: epoiēsen hēmas basileian hiereis: 'made us a kingdom, priests.' The aorist epoiēsen marks this as accomplished fact, not future aspiration. The construction alludes to Exodus 19:6 LXX (basileion hierateuma, royal priesthood) — what Israel was called to be at Sinai has been constituted in Christ. The MKT follows the reading basileian hiereis (a kingdom, priests) rather than basileion hierateuma (royal priesthood), following Codex Alexandrinus; the meaning is the same: priestly mediation joined to royal authority.
7: idou erchetai meta tōn nephelōn: present tense coming — not 'will come' (eleusetai) but 'is coming' (erchetai), the same present used of Christ's coming throughout Revelation; the parousia is in motion. Kopsetai ep auton pasai hai phylai tēs gēs: 'all the tribes of the earth will mourn/wail for him' — kopsomai is the word for funeral lamentation; Zechariah 12:10-12 uses it for the mourning over the pierced one. Nai kai amēn: double affirmation, combining Greek (nai) and Hebrew/Aramaic (amēn) for emphatic certainty.
9: synkoinōnos en tē thlipsei kai basileia kai hypomonē: 'fellow-partaker in the affliction and kingdom and patient endurance.' The three nouns are linked by a single article (tē): affliction, kingdom, and endurance are a single package — the kingdom is already present, but the path through it requires endurance of affliction. Hypomonē (patient endurance) is not passive waiting but active staying under pressure. John is in thlipsis (affliction) on Patmos because of witness — not despite his relationship to the kingdom but as the form that kingdom-life takes under opposition.
10: egenomēn en pneumati en tē kyriakē hēmera: 'I came to be in the Spirit on the Lord's day.' Egenomēn (aorist): a moment of transition — John entered a state of Spirit-possession. Kyriakē hēmera (Lord's Day) is the first occurrence of this phrase in the NT; it identifies Sunday as the day of Christian assembly in commemoration of the resurrection. Phōnēn opisthen mou hōs salpiggos: voice behind him like a trumpet — the spatial disorientation (behind, not in front) and the trumpet sound signal divine address; compare Exodus 19:16 (trumpet sound at Sinai).
11: ho blepeis grapson eis biblion kai pempson tais hepta ekklēsiais: 'What you see, write into a scroll and send to the seven churches.' The commission is visual-to-written: John is commanded to transcribe his vision (ho blepeis, what you are seeing) as a letter to seven named communities. The seven churches are historically identifiable cities in Roman Asia (modern western Turkey) along a roughly circular mail route — the Revelation was a circular letter intended to be read in each assembly.
12: hepta lychnias chrysas: seven golden lampstands. The lampstand (lychnia) is the menorah-form of the tabernacle and temple (Exodus 25:31-40), redeployed here for seven separate churches. Christ walking en mesō tōn hepta lychniōn (in the midst of the seven lampstands) recalls the priestly role of tending the menorah — Christ as the heavenly priest maintaining/attending to the churches, his seven-fold presence among them.
13: homoion huion anthrōpou: 'like a son of man' — homoion + accusative, not the full Danielic title but the allusive vision-form (Daniel 7:13; Revelation 10:16). The description that follows combines Daniel's heavenly figure with Ezekiel's: endedymenon podērē (clothed to the feet: the priestly/royal long robe), periezōsmenon pros tois mastois zonēn chrysēn (girded at the chest with a golden sash — the position of the sash shifts from the waist, where soldiers wear it, to the chest, the position of seated authority or priestly service).
14: hē kephalē autou kai hai triches leukai hōs erion leukon hōs chiōn: head and hair white as wool, white as snow — drawn directly from the Ancient of Days in Daniel 7:9 (the divine throne-figure with white like wool). By applying this description to the glorified Christ, Revelation identifies Jesus with the divine throne-figure of Daniel. Hoi ophthalmoi autou hōs phlox pyros (eyes like a flame of fire): from Daniel 10:6, the heavenly man with eyes like torches of fire — omniscience and penetrating judgment.
15: hoi podes autou homoioi chalkolibanō hōs en kaminos pepyrōmenēs: feet like burnished bronze/electrum, as if refined in a furnace — from Daniel 10:6 and Ezekiel 1:7 (gleaming bronze). Chalkolibanos is a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly meaning a precious brass alloy or incense-copper; its exact identification is uncertain, but the refined-in-fire imagery signals purification and solidity. Kai hē phōnē autou hōs phōnē hydatōn pollōn: voice like many waters — Ezekiel 43:2's description of God's voice returning to the restored temple.
16: echōn en tē dexia cheiri autou asteras hepta kai ek tou stomatos autou rhomphaia distomos oxeia ekporeuomenē: holding seven stars in his right hand; from his mouth a sharp double-edged sword. Rhomphaia (the large Thracian sword, a broad-bladed weapon) rather than machaira (the short sword) — Revelation consistently uses rhomphaia for the sword from Christ's mouth (Revelation 1:16; Revelation 2:12, 16; Revelation 19:15, 21), perhaps to emphasize its imposing, judicial weight. The sword from the mouth = the word of divine judgment: Christ's weapon is speech, not physical force.
18: echō tas kleidas tou thanatou kai tou Hadou: 'I hold the keys of Death and Hades.' Echō (present tense): Christ currently and continuously possesses these keys — this is his present status as the risen one. Death and Hades are personified as a paired power throughout Revelation (Revelation 6:8; Revelation 20:13-14), functioning as the last enemies; to hold their keys is to control entry and exit from the realm of the dead. The resurrection is not just Christ's personal survival but his seizure of authority over the entire structure of mortality.
19: grapson oun ha eideskon kai ha eisin kai ha mellei genesthai meta tauta: 'Write therefore what you saw and what is and what is to take place after this.' The three-part formula has generated debate about Revelation's structure: (1) what you saw = Revelation 1 vision; (2) what is = Revelation 2-3 (the churches as they presently are); (3) what is to come = Revelation 4-22. Ha mellei genesthai: the events that are about to happen — mellei (is about to) again signals imminence.
20: to mystērion tōn hepta asterōn... kai tas hepta lychnias tas chrysas: 'the mystery of the seven stars... and the seven golden lampstands.' Mystērion (mystery): a secret that has been disclosed — the vision's imagery is explained by Christ himself. The seven stars are angeloi (messengers/angels) of the seven churches — whether guardian angels, heavenly representatives, or human leaders (bishops/overseers) is debated; the usage allows both. The lampstands are the churches themselves, visible light-bearers in the world, tended by the one who walks among them.
